aliases: Deep-fried egg tofu with vegetables and minced pork

english: Deep-fried egg tofu with vegetables and minced pork

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for hot plate tofu; closest defensible match is tofu-based dish (FOODON_03315671). Egg tofu with mixed vegetables and minced pork on a sizzling iron plate is a Singapore-specific preparation not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Tofu and tofu products) is the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for hot plate egg tofu with minced pork and oyster sauce as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hot plate tofu as a specific food concept. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dish variants at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hot plate tofu at a Singapore hawker centre or zi char stall is approximately 250 g, comprising several pieces of deep-fried egg tofu, a portion of mixed vegetables, minced pork, and oyster sauce served on a sizzling iron plate.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Plate Tofu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hot Plate Tofu (cr_id F09050007). All core macros, secondary nutrients, sodium, calcium, and dietary fibre are taken from this record. Iron was reported as -1 (not available) in the HPB source.

gi_source.title: Tofu, plain — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load values from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008); analogous estimate applied to egg tofu preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot plate egg tofu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to plain tofu (GI ~15, low category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables. Carbohydrate content is 0.9 g/100g; glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 0.9 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 0.3, confirming negligible glycemic impact. The deep-frying and oyster sauce addition do not materially alter the GI of the tofu base given the dominant fat and protein matrix.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100g from soy sauce and oyster sauce with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g per meal and request reduced-sodium soy sauce and oyster sauce; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, approaching 40% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.9 g/100g) but high fat (17.1 g/100g) may slow gastric emptying and alter post-prandial glucose patterns
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot plate tofu is low in carbohydrates and has a low glycemic index (~15), making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content (17.1 g/100g) from deep-frying may delay gastric emptying and affect post-meal glucose patterns, so portion control and monitoring of individual glycaemic response are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.1 g/100g) from deep-frying and elevated sodium (380 mg/100g) both contribute to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume hot plate tofu sparingly due to its high fat content from deep-frying and elevated sodium from oyster and soy sauces; requesting steamed tofu with reduced sauce and smaller portions significantly lowers the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium 380 mg/100g approaches RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hot plate tofu is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the deep-frying preparation method generating AGEs that promote joint inflammation; a steamed or pan-fried tofu alternative with reduced sauce is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Tofu (铁板豆腐, Tauhu Piring Panas) is a popular Singaporean Chinese zi char dish consisting of deep-fried egg tofu pieces served on a sizzling cast-iron plate, topped with stir-fried mixed vegetables, minced pork, and a savoury oyster sauce and soy sauce glaze. The egg tofu is silky and custard-like on the inside with a lightly crisp exterior from deep-frying, while the sizzling iron plate keeps the dish hot and imparts a slight caramelised aroma to the sauce. The dish is commonly found at hawker centres and zi char stalls across Singapore and is typically ordered as a shared dish in a multi-dish meal. Nutritionally, hot plate tofu is energy-dense at 188 kcal/100g, driven primarily by its high fat content of 17.1 g/100g from the deep-frying process. Protein is moderate at 7.4 g/100g from the tofu and minced pork. Carbohydrates are very low at 0.9 g/100g with negligible sugar, giving the dish a low glycemic index (~15) and minimal glycemic load. Sodium is elevated at 380 mg/100g due to oyster sauce and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Calcium is present at 30 mg/100g from the tofu base. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the minced pork and oyster sauce. It is not halal due to pork content. Clinically, the dish is low-GI and low-carbohydrate, which is favourable for glycaemic management, but the high fat and sodium content require portion control for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot plate tofu?
Is hot plate tofu suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat hot plate tofu if I have high blood pressure?
Is sizzling tofu high in sodium?
What is the GI of tofu dishes?
How much protein is in hot plate tofu?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09050007), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the source system. GI is estimated by analogy to plain tofu from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; this introduces a minor uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the very low carbohydrate content and well-established tofu GI literature.
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hot Plate Tofu (F09050007) does not report an iron value for this preparation; limited published iron data exist for this specific deep-fried egg tofu with minced pork and oyster sauce combination. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific hot plate tofu preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source); this is plausible given the minimal carbohydrate content and the fact that oyster sauce sugar contribution is negligible at the dish level as reported. Dietary tags from input (vegetarian, plant_based, keto) have been corrected: this dish contains minced pork and oyster sauce and is therefore not vegetarian or plant-based; keto tag is retained as the carbohydrate content is 0.9 g/100g. is_halal set to false due to pork content. GI estimated by analogy to plain tofu (GI ~15) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for hot plate egg tofu exists in approved sources.
